---
title: "Assignment II: Group [D]"
author: "Cheng Nok Ho"
format: 
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: false
    embed-resources: true # <= check here: https://quarto.org/docs/reference/formats/html.html
execute:
  echo: true    # <= knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE) in Rmarkdown
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Name

[*Cheng Nok Ho]{style="color:red"}: 21062910*

# Generative AI declaration

::: callout
During the assignment, I used Deep Seek to help understand plotly and gganimate syntax. I subsequently reviewed and edited the code and take(s) full responsibility for the final version of the code.”
:::


# Dataset

Each group member may choose either of the following dataset:

*Built-in dataset*

-   palmerpenguins::penguins
-   ggplot2::msleep
-   ggplot2::midwest
-   datasets::quakes

*Public dataset*

BIOTIME (CC0) <https://biotime.st-andrews.ac.uk/usageGuidelines.php>

-   BIOTIME_raw_data_192.csv (uploaded on Canvas) (Whale data from <https://biotime.st-andrews.ac.uk/selectStudy.php?study=192>)
-   BIOTIME_raw_data_232.csv (uploaded on Canvas) (Fish data from <https://biotime.st-andrews.ac.uk/selectStudy.php?study=232>)

# Figures by each group member

### Dataset

::: callout-important
[Which dataset did you choose?]{style="color:darkred; font-weight:bold;"}
:::

::: callout
### Your answer is here
ggplot2::msleep
:::

### Introduction

::: callout-important
[What is the dataset about? What variables are included in the data? How was the data taken/measured?]{style="color:darkred; font-weight:bold;"}
:::

::: callout
### Your answer is here
The 'ggplot2::msleep' dataset contains sleep and weight information for 83 different mammals. There are 11 variables for each animal, including: 
name (species), genus, vore (diet type), order (biological order), conservation (conservation status), sleep_total (total sleep hours per day), sleep_rem (REM sleep hours), sleep_cycle, awake (hours awake), brainwt (brain weight in kg), and bodywt (body weight in kg).

The dataset plays a crucial role in learning and practing a professional data science workflow. It was obtained through direct observation using electroencephalography (EEG) and behavioural monitoring.
:::

### Interactive plot(s) using plotly

::: callout-important
[Please explain which plot type you choose and why. Also, please discuss the strengths and weaknesses of your plot(s).]{style="color:darkred; font-weight:bold;"}
:::

::: callout
### Your answer is here
i chose an interactive scatterplot because it shows relationships between two continuous variables, body weight and total sleep hours.

Strengths: easy to reveal correlation between two variables, preserves individual data point

Weaknesses: not suitable for static reports, overlapping points may occur
:::

Note: At least one plot is required, but multiple plots are allowed

::: callout-important
[What is the purpose of this visualization?]{style="color:darkred; font-weight:bold;"}
:::

::: callout
### Your answer is here
The purpose of the visualization is to show how total daily sleep hours varies with body wright accross different mammals. 
:::

Note: If you want to specify setting for a code chunk, you can use #| for each option (for example, #| echo: true). Rmarkdown code chunk still works (for example, {r echo = true}), but Quarto style is recommended.

{r}
#| echo: true
# YOUR CODE HERE
library(ggplot2)
library(plotly)
data(msleep)

p_static <- ggplot(msleep, aes(x = bodywt, y = sleep_total, color = order,
                               text = paste("Species:", name, 
                                            "<br>Order:", order, 
                                            "<br>Brain weight (kg):", brainwt))) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Mammalian sleep total vs body weight",
       x = "Body weight (kg)",
       y = "Total sleep (hours/day)") +
  theme_minimal()

ggplotly(p_static, tooltip = "text")


### Animation using gganimate

::: callout-important
[Please explain which animation type you choose and why. Also, please discuss the strengths and weaknesses of your animation.]{style="color:darkred; font-weight:bold;"}
:::

::: callout
### Your answer is here
I chose a time-based animation using transition_time() on sleep_total.

Strengths: clear to reveal gaps in data which highlights data sparsity

Weaknesses: while sleep_total is not actual time, it may be conceptually confusing to be representing real-world time progression rather than a ranked ordering
:::

Note: At least one animation is required, but multiple animations are allowed

::: callout-important
[What is the purpose of this visualization?]{style="color:darkred; font-weight:bold;"}
:::

::: callout
### Your answer is here
The purpose of the visualization is to animate mammls ordered by total sleep hours, illustrating the range of sleep durations across different species.
:::

{r}
# YOUR CODE HERE
#| echo: true
library(gganimate)
library(gifski)
data(msleep)

p_anim <- ggplot(msleep, aes(x = bodywt, y = sleep_total, color = order)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Mammals ordered by sleep duration",
       x = "Body weight (kg)",
       y = "Total sleep (hours/day)") +
  transition_time(sleep_total) +
  shadow_wake(wake_length = 0.3) +
  theme_minimal()

animate(p_anim, nframes = 100, fps = 8, width = 800, height = 500)
